output_text:
Revised the draft to eliminate pervasive self-repetition, tighten phrasing, and improve flow while preserving every citation, number, and technical claim.

---

In this section we present our methodology for cross-validating exoplanet candidate detections, which addresses the false-positive contamination problem that affects transit surveys such as Kepler and TESS. Our approach combines vetting signals from multiple independent classifiers to improve overall reliability.

The pipeline ingests light curves from the TESS Science Processing Operations Center (SPOC) pipeline (Jenkins et al. 2016) and applies the Box Least Squares (BLS) periodogram (Kovács et al. 2002) to identify candidate transit signatures. Candidates are then fit with a Mandel & Agol (2002) transit model — parameterized by planet-to-star radius ratio, impact parameter, and quadratic limb-darkening coefficients — using the affine-invariant ensemble sampler emcee (Foreman-Mackey et al. 2013).

Signals that survive the BLS+MCMC stage are passed to three independent classifier agents: (i) a centroid-shift discriminator following Bryson et al. (2013), (ii) an odd-even transit-depth comparator, and (iii) a Gaussian-process variability test built on the celerite framework (Foreman-Mackey et al. 2017). Each agent produces a reliability score, and the three scores are combined through a weighted consensus rule.

We evaluated the pipeline on the TESS Object of Interest (TOI) catalog, restricted to TOIs with confirmed dispositions in the NASA Exoplanet Archive. The pipeline recovers 92% of confirmed planets while flagging 87% of known false positives — recovery and false-positive rates competitive with previous vetting frameworks such as Robovetter (Thompson et al. 2018) and DAVE (Kostov et al. 2019).

---

Key changes:
- Removed triple-stated thesis in paragraph 1 (kept one clean statement).
- Collapsed the "Mandel & Agol provides analytical light-curve shapes... these analytical light-curve shapes are fit using emcee, which is..." loop into a single sentence with an em-dash parenthetical.
- Cut "Each of these three classifier agents produces... and the three classifier agents independently score..." duplication; used a numbered list to make the three vetters scannable.
- Replaced the "recovers a substantial majority... flagging the majority..." restatement (which just paraphrases the 92%/87% numbers) with a direct transition to the benchmark comparison.
- Minor: "transit-depth comparator" hyphenated for clarity; "celerite framework" kept as-is per the cited package name.

Uncertainty disclosure: I did not verify the cited references or the 92%/87% figures — those are preserved verbatim from your draft and should be checked against your actual results.
